hq: San Francisco, California (no physical office; operates from co-working space)

system_description.overview: AfterQuery is a research-driven AI data provider that helps organizations obtain high-quality, human-generated datasets that cannot be found online or reliably produced synthetically. The company focuses on creating and curating specialized data to improve model performance, safety, and reliability. Backed by Y Combinator (W25).

products.Data Programs Platform: Managed workflows to specify, produce, and quality-control human-generated datasets. Supports SFT pairs, RLHF preference data, computer-use/agent trajectories, and RL environments. Includes multi-reviewer QC, rubric scoring, leakage/bias screens, audit trails, and dataset versioning.
products.Evaluation & Safety Toolkit: Evaluation harness templates (pass@k, failure taxonomies, red-team suites), acceptance thresholds, scheduled reporting dashboards, and optional integration with customer endpoints / air-gapped runs.
products.Research Dataset Library: Curated pre-built datasets in multimodal, computer-use/agent workflows, and alignment/safety areas with documentation and license/usage terms.
products.Enterprise Consulting: Advisory/embedded services for AI program strategy, KPIs, evaluation/safety design, pipeline/architecture, governance, and enablement.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) - carve-out (subservice organization, controls excluded)
infrastructure.physical_presence: None - no physical offices or servers; all cloud-based
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (multiple availability zones within us-east1 confirmed in BCDR section)
infrastructure.cdn: Vercel Edge (implied by Next.js + Edge frontend deployment)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Segmented VPCs with access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality (CC6.1.7).
network_architecture.api_gateway: Cloud Run (API REST layer visible in network diagram)
network_architecture.waf: Firewalls configured on application gateway and production network (CC6.6.1). Specific WAF product not named.
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management (CC6.6.1).
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS employed via GCP to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access through bastion host with VPN.
network_architecture.tls: TLS encryption for all user requests to web-based systems, using certificates from established third-party certificate authority. HTTPS enforced on all endpoints (QA and production).
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems restricted to internet with content filtering system routed through proxy server.
network_architecture.topology: Internet -> End Users -> HTTPS (QA) / HTTPS -> Vercel Frontend (Next.js + Edge) -> Sign-in/JWT -> Firebase Auth -> API (REST) -> Cloud Run Backend (HTTPS) -> Firestore Database + Cloud Storage (GCS) Buckets. API calls also route to LLM Providers (Anthropic API, OpenAI API). DevOps: Linear (Tasks) -> PR links -> GitHub (Main CI/CD) -> Deploy (staging/prod). Slack for team comms and log alerting. Two environments: Staging (us-east1) and Production (us-east1).

application_architecture.environments: Two environments: Staging and Production, both in GCP us-east1. Changes tested in staging before production deployment (CC8.1.4).

data_storage.backup_frequency: Daily (CC7.4.2: backups taken daily, multi-AZ configured)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Firebase Auth (Sign-in/JWT visible in network diagram)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for: administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1), source code tool access (CC8.1.3), all remote access (CC6.1.1 test), and employee email access.
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination (CC6.1.4)
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through GCP password policy with periodic forced changes, password expiry, and complexity requirements. Accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts. Guest/anonymous logins not allowed. Unattended desktops lock after inactivity.

encryption.in_transit: TLS (version not specified; certificates from third-party CA)
encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service (CC6.1.5: organization uses cloud provider KMS to encrypt data at rest)
encryption.key_management: GCP Cloud KMS (CC6.1.5: cloud provider key management service to store and manage encryption keys; access to keys restricted to authorized individuals - CC6.1.9)

ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: GitHub CI/CD (visible in network diagram as 'GitHub Main CI/CD')
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to move change to production requires additional approval (CC8.1.2). Approval process followed prior to merging to master branch.
ci_cd_devops.iac: Infrastructure as code implied (baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code, configuration management tool used - CC7.1.1). Specific tool not named.
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management with monthly patch management reviews by CTO. Workstations configured for automatic updates, scanned daily for patch compliance (CC7.5.1).

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not specifically named; monitoring tools generate alerts on predefined thresholds (CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Not specifically named; logging enabled to monitor administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level (CC7.2.1)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Slack (log alerting visible in network diagram); automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events (CC7.2.1)

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure / subservice organization for data center services
LLM API provider (API calls from backend)
LLM API provider (API calls from backend)

security_tools.antivirus: Endpoint Security Solution with automatic scanning. Anti-virus installed on all desktops, laptops, and production servers. Virus definitions updated daily. Anti-virus definitions on production servers updated weekly. Mac-specific malware protection also deployed.
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications (CC7.1.2). Frequency adjusted as required. Internal IT Infrastructure team performs scans. Self-assessment scan prior to every release or yearly.
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party (CC4.1.1). Results reviewed by IT management. High/critical findings remediated and added to risk register.

bcdr.rto: Not disclosed (DR plan identifies time frames based on business impact analysis but specific RTO not stated)

untestable: CC2.2.3 - Significant changes to people, roles and responsibilities: could not be tested because there were no significant changes made during the engagement period
CC3.2.1 - Cybersecurity insurance: could not be tested because there were no cyber incidents reported during the engagement period
CC6.5.2 - Customer data deletion upon termination: could not be tested because there were no customer terminations during the engagement period
CC7.3.1 - Incident response (test results): could not be tested because there were no security incidents reported during the engagement period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access to facilities: GCP is responsible for physical security of data center; not in scope
A1.2 - Environmental protections: Afterquery operates from co-working space; environmental controls managed by building management / GCP
Processing Integrity - Determined not relevant to the system
Privacy - Determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC8.1.2 - Branch protection enforced: developers cannot make changes to production without additional approval via merge request
CC8.1.3 - Source code access restricted with MFA to authorized users
CC6.1.7 - Network segmentation implemented based on sensitivity and criticality
CC7.4.2 - Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration and alerting for backup failures
CC6.1.3 - Customer access appropriately segmented from other customer accounts
CC2.1.2 - RGEC (Risk and Governance Executive Committee) meets semiannually to review internal controls
CC3.1.1 - IT Leadership Committee meets weekly to review security activities and processes

infrastructure.rationale: Single region (us-east1) on GCP with multi-AZ within that region. Serverless compute via Cloud Run and Vercel Edge is modern and scalable. However, no multi-region redundancy, no named CDN, and the carve-out approach to GCP means less visibility into infrastructure controls. IaC mentioned generically but no specific tool named.

application_architecture.rationale: Modern stack with Next.js frontend on Vercel Edge and Cloud Run serverless backend. REST API style. Two separated environments with branch protection and code review required. However, only one language/framework ecosystem visible, and no mention of automated testing, CI pipeline details, or containerization specifics beyond Cloud Run.

data_layer.rationale: Firestore (NoSQL) and Cloud Storage buckets provide basic data layer. Daily backups with multi-AZ configured. Data classification and retention policies in place. Per-tenant segregation confirmed. However, no caching layer, no data warehouse, no secondary database types mentioned, and no specific backup restoration testing described.

security_posture.rationale: Strong security posture: MFA enforced broadly, IDS/IPS via GCP, firewalls with annual review, annual external pen testing, monthly vulnerability scanning, endpoint protection with anti-malware on all devices, server hardening, cybersecurity insurance, data encryption at rest and in transit via KMS. Content filtering through proxy. VPN + bastion host for remote access. Minor gaps: no named SIEM or specific security tooling vendors, TLS version not specified.

devops_maturity.rationale: GitHub for source control and CI/CD with branch protection rules enforced. Separate staging and production environments. Formal change management with emergency change process. IaC mentioned but tool unnamed. Automatic patch management with monthly reviews. Linear for task tracking. Missing: specific CI/CD pipeline details, container registry info, automated test coverage metrics.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing of DR plans across different loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover with daily backups and alerting. DR plan identifies critical systems and time frames based on business impact analysis. However, no stated RTO/RPO, no multi-region failover, and specific recovery testing results not detailed.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Good vendor diversity with 8+ identifiable vendors across different functions: GCP (infra), Vercel (frontend), Firebase (auth/DB), Anthropic + OpenAI (LLM), GitHub (DevOps), Linear (PM), Slack (comms). Dual LLM provider strategy reduces single-vendor AI dependency. Primary concentration risk is GCP ecosystem (compute + storage + DB + auth all Google). Vendor management policy with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports.

overall.rationale: AfterQuery demonstrates a solid baseline security and compliance posture for an early-stage startup (YC W25). Clean audit opinion with zero exceptions across 55 tested controls. Modern serverless architecture on GCP. Key strengths include comprehensive access controls, annual pen testing, daily backups, and formal BCDR. Key gaps include single-region deployment, limited disclosure of specific tooling (no named SIEM, APM, or IaC tool), no stated RTO/RPO, and a 3-month audit period suggesting this is their first SOC 2.

red_flags: Single-region deployment (us-east1 only) creates geographic concentration risk with no multi-region failover
Very short audit period (3 months: May-Aug 2025) suggests first-ever SOC 2, limiting confidence in control maturity
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria excluded as 'not relevant' despite handling sensitive AI training data and human-generated datasets
No RTO/RPO disclosed despite having a DR plan, making recovery expectations unclear
yellow_flags: GCP carve-out means critical infrastructure controls are excluded from audit scope
No named SIEM, APM, or log aggregation tool - monitoring capabilities may be basic
IaC tool not specified despite claiming infrastructure-as-code practices
No caching layer or data warehouse mentioned, potentially limiting platform scalability
4 controls could not be tested due to no events during the short engagement period (no incidents, no terminations, no significant personnel changes, no cyber insurance claims)
TLS version not specified - could be using older protocols
Cybersecurity insurance control untestable (no incidents) - unclear if policy is actually in force or just planned
green_flags: Clean unqualified opinion with zero exceptions across all testable controls
MFA enforced comprehensively: production access, source code, remote access, email
Modern serverless architecture (Cloud Run + Vercel Edge) reduces infrastructure management burden
Dual LLM provider strategy (Anthropic + OpenAI) avoids single-vendor AI dependency
Branch protection with mandatory code review for production deployments
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration and automated failure alerting
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation tracking
Formal vendor management program with annual SOC report reviews
RGEC (Risk and Governance Executive Committee) provides board-level security oversight
Y Combinator backing (W25) indicates external validation of business model
Per-tenant data segregation for customer accounts
Comprehensive endpoint protection including Mac-specific malware protection
key_observations: Network diagram reveals LLM API calls to both Anthropic and OpenAI from the backend, indicating the platform actively uses third-party AI models as part of its service delivery - this creates data flow paths where customer data may transit through LLM providers
Firebase Auth + Firestore + GCS creates deep Google ecosystem lock-in beyond just GCP compute, making migration significantly harder than a typical cloud-hosted app
The company operates without physical offices (co-working space), which simplifies physical security but also means all security controls are logical/digital - a fully remote workforce model
Signed URLs mentioned as optional in the network diagram for object storage access, suggesting some data delivery may use time-limited direct-access tokens rather than API-mediated access

Despite being an AI data company handling sensitive training data (including RLHF data from real users), Privacy trust criteria were excluded from scope - this may be a concern for enterprise customers

The org chart is flat: CEO -> CTO + COO -> 4 teams (Software Engineering, Research, Strategic Project Leads, Growth). This lean structure is typical for early-stage but raises key-person dependency risks
Linear for task management + Slack for alerting suggests lightweight tooling stack, but no dedicated incident management or on-call platform (e.g., PagerDuty, OpsGenie) was identified
